left-join-preserves-unmatched-rows.label: LEFT JOIN Preserves Unmatched Rows
left-join-preserves-unmatched-rows.summary: Learn how LEFT JOIN can be used to preserve all rows from a starting table, even when there are no matching records in the joined table.

sketch1.title: Preserve the Starting Entity Across a Longer Path

try_left_join_preserves_unmatched_rows_s.title: Try LEFT JOIN with Every Department
try_left_join_preserves_unmatched_rows_s.prompt: List every department beside a related course id. Preserve departments that do not offer a course, so `Arts` still appears with a NULL course id.
try_left_join_preserves_unmatched_rows_s.hint: Place the table that must be preserved on the left side of the join.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN preserves every row from the left table even when the selected right-side value is missing.
help.hint_2: Select `departments.name` and `courses.id`; this is a different relationship and result from the lesson's student example.

try_left_join_preserves_unmatched_rows_s.solutionCode:
SELECT departments.name, courses.id
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses ON departments.id = courses.department_id
ORDER BY departments.id, courses.id;

try_left_join_preserves_unmatched_rows_s.title: Try Preserving Departments through a Longer Path
try_left_join_preserves_unmatched_rows_s.prompt: List every department with the names of students enrolled in its courses. Preserve departments with no courses or enrollments, so `Arts` remains visible.
try_left_join_preserves_unmatched_rows_s.hint: Keep every join after `departments` as a LEFT JOIN so a missing row does not break the path.

help.concept: Preservation can continue across a multi-table path when every optional relationship uses LEFT JOIN.
help.hint_2: Select department and student names; the lesson example instead starts from students and returns course titles.

try_left_join_preserves_unmatched_rows_s.solutionCode:
SELECT departments.name AS department_name,
       students.name AS student_name
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses ON departments.id = courses.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id
LEFT JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.id
ORDER BY departments.id, students.name;

multi-choice-1.title: Understanding LEFT JOIN
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following statements about LEFT JOIN is true?
multi-choice-1.hint: Consider how LEFT JOIN handles unmatched rows.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN includes all rows from the left table and matched rows from the right table.
help.hint_1: LEFT JOIN will include unmatched rows from the left table with NULLs for the right table columns.
help.hint_2: LEFT JOIN is useful for preserving all rows from the left table.

options.a: LEFT JOIN only includes rows with matches in both tables.
options.b: LEFT JOIN includes all rows from the left table, even if there are no matches in the right table.
options.c: LEFT JOIN excludes unmatched rows from the left table.
options.d: LEFT JOIN is the same as INNER JOIN.

multi-choice-2.title: LEFT JOIN vs INNER JOIN
multi-choice-2.prompt: What is the main difference between LEFT JOIN and INNER JOIN?
multi-choice-2.hint: Think about how unmatched rows are handled in each join type.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN includes unmatched rows from the left table, while INNER JOIN does not.
help.hint_1: LEFT JOIN preserves all rows from the left table, even if there are no matches.
help.hint_2: INNER JOIN only includes rows with matches in both tables.

options.a: LEFT JOIN includes unmatched rows from the left table, INNER JOIN does not.
options.b: INNER JOIN includes unmatched rows from the left table, LEFT JOIN does not.
options.c: Both LEFT JOIN and INNER JOIN include unmatched rows from the left table.
options.d: Neither LEFT JOIN nor INNER JOIN include unmatched rows from the left table.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of SQL Clauses
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the SQL clauses in the correct order for a LEFT JOIN query.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

fill-blank-1.title: LEFT JOIN Syntax
fill-blank-1.prompt: Complete the LEFT JOIN syntax: `SELECT * FROM students [blank1] enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;`
fill-blank-1.hint: The missing keyword is used to join tables while preserving all rows from the left table.

help.concept: the missing term is used to include all rows from the left table, even if there are no matches in the right table.
help.hint_1: The keyword is used to join tables while keeping all rows from the left table.
help.hint_2: The keyword is the missing term.

fill-blank-1.template: SELECT * FROM students [blank1] enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;

fill-blank-2.title: LEFT JOIN Result
fill-blank-2.prompt: In a LEFT JOIN, unmatched rows from the left table will have [blank1] in the columns from the right table.
fill-blank-2.hint: Consider what value is used when there is no match in the right table.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN results in the missing term for unmatched rows from the left table in the right table's columns.
help.hint_1: Unmatched rows from the left table will have the missing term in the right table's columns.
help.hint_2: The value is the missing term.

fill-blank-2.template: In a LEFT JOIN, unmatched rows from the left table will have [blank1] in the columns from the right table.

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of LEFT JOIN
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of using a LEFT JOIN in SQL?
single-choice-1.hint: Choose the table whose rows must all remain.

help.concept: LEFT JOIN preserves every row from the chosen left table and fills missing right-side values with NULL.
help.hint_1: Identify which table the report says must remain complete.
help.hint_2: Start from that table and check the unmatched row in the results after running the query.

options.a: To include only matching rows from both tables.
options.b: To include all rows from the left table, even if there are no matches in the right table.
options.c: To exclude unmatched rows from the left table.
options.d: To include all rows from the right table, even if there are no matches in the left table.

sketch0.title: LEFT JOIN preserves every row from its left table
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A **LEFT JOIN** keeps every row from the table written on the left side of the join, whether or not a matching row exists on the right. When no match exists, SQL preserves the left row and fills the right-side columns with `NULL`.

```sql
SELECT students.name, enrollments.course_id
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments
  ON students.id = enrollments.student_id;
```

The query begins with `students`, so every student is promised a place in the result. Students with enrollments produce matched rows. A student with no enrollment still appears once, but `enrollments.course_id` is `NULL` because there is no related enrollment value to display.

This is the central difference from `INNER JOIN`. An inner join asks for existing pairs and removes unmatched rows. A left join begins with a reporting requirement: **keep all of these rows**, then attach related information where it exists.

The table order therefore carries meaning. If the report must include every department, put `departments` on the left. If it must include every student, put `students` on the left. Choose the preserved entity before writing the join.

sketch1.title: Every later LEFT JOIN must continue the same promise
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Preserving a starting table through several relationships requires a chain of `LEFT JOIN`s. If the path begins with students, each later join must allow the unmatched student row—with its growing set of `NULL` values—to continue.

```sql
SELECT students.name, courses.title
FROM students
LEFT JOIN enrollments
  ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
LEFT JOIN courses
  ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id;
```

First, every student survives the join to `enrollments`. For a student without an enrollment, `enrollments.course_id` becomes `NULL`. The second left join cannot find a course for that `NULL` id, so `courses.title` also becomes `NULL`; the original student row remains.

If the second join were changed to `INNER JOIN`, the unmatched student would disappear at that point. The query would quietly break the promise made by the first join.

Read a longer outer-join path from the starting entity outward. At every step, ask whether the report still contains the row it was designed to preserve. The `NULL` values are not missing output to hide—they are accurate evidence that the relationship ends there.
